notes.anchor: go on from Lanuvium to the Tusculanum
notes.note: The Tusculanum was Cicero's favourite villa in the Alban hills, the setting of much of his philosophical writing and full of memories of Tullia. Returning to it is the ordeal he is steeling himself to face here, reasoning that the grief will follow him wherever he goes.

notes.anchor: To a cultivated mind nothing is uncouth, nothing inhuman
notes.note: A near-proverbial maxim in the consolatory tradition: a mind trained by philosophy can absorb any blow without becoming brutalized. Cicero offers it half as self-counsel against the fear that grief, or its remedies, will coarsen him.
